(French: *Confédération canadienne*) was the process by which three [British North American](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") provinces—the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick")—were united into one [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation"), called the [Dominion](/wiki/Name_of_Canada#Adoption_of_Dominion "Name of Canada") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), on July 1, 1867.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin19951-2) This process occurred with the rising tide of [Canadian nationalism](/wiki/Canadian_nationalism "Canadian nationalism") that was then beginning to swell within these provinces and others. It reached fruition through the *British North America Act, 1867* (today known as the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*) which had been based on resolutions agreed to by colonial delegates in the [1864 Quebec Conference](/wiki/Quebec_Conference,_1864 "Quebec Conference, 1864"), later finalized in the [1866 London Conference](/wiki/London_Conference_of_1866 "London Conference of 1866").

**Canadian Confederation** (French: *Confédération canadienne*) was the process by which three [British North American](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America") provinces—the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), and [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick")—were united into one [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation"), called the [Dominion](/wiki/Name_of_Canada#Adoption_of_Dominion "Name of Canada") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), on July 1, 1867.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartin19951-2) This process occurred with the rising tide of [Canadian nationalism](/wiki/Canadian_nationalism "Canadian nationalism") that was then beginning to swell within these provinces and others. It reached fruition through the *British North America Act, 1867* (today known as the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*) which had been based on resolutions agreed to by colonial delegates in the [1864 Quebec Conference](/wiki/Quebec_Conference,_1864 "Quebec Conference, 1864"), later finalized in the [1866 London Conference](/wiki/London_Conference_of_1866 "London Conference of 1866").

Upon Confederation, Canada consisted of four provinces: [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), which had been split out from the Province of Canada, and the provinces of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The province of [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"), which had hosted the first meeting to consider Confederation, the [Charlottetown Conference](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference"), did not join Confederation until 1873. Over the years since Confederation, Canada has seen numerous territorial changes and expansions, resulting in the current collection of [ten provinces and three territories](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada").

Political impasse in the Province of Canada and the loss of preferential access to U.S. markets after Washington cancelled the [Canadian–American Reciprocity Treaty](/wiki/Canadian%E2%80%93American_Reciprocity_Treaty "Canadian–American Reciprocity Treaty") in 1866 sharpened the colonies' sense of economic vulnerability and motivated the desire for federal unification and market integration. The leaders of the [Maritime colonies](/wiki/The_Maritimes "The Maritimes") pressed for Ottawa's assumption of their public debts and for an [intercolonial railway](/wiki/Intercolonial_Railway "Intercolonial Railway") that would bind the trade of the [St. Lawrence](/wiki/St._Lawrence_River "St. Lawrence River") to an ice-free Atlantic port, while politicians across [Canada West](/wiki/Canada_West "Canada West") and [Canada East](/wiki/Canada_East "Canada East") saw federation as the only way to break legislative deadlock and finance large-scale infrastructure. At the same time, lingering fears of the U.S. concept of [manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny"), memories of the [Fenian raids](/wiki/Fenian_raids "Fenian raids"), and [Britain's desire to off-load](/wiki/Little_Englander "Little Englander") defence costs persuaded many that a larger fiscal and military union offered the surest bulwark against American pressure and metropolitan indifference. The motto "[peace, order, and good government](/wiki/Peace,_order,_and_good_government "Peace, order, and good government")" arose as an expression of a distinctly Canadian formulation of constitutional government in North America.

While historians have often portrayed Confederation as having emerged from pragmatic and administrative rationales, recent scholarship has uncovered a rich contest of ideas beneath the politicking, involving competing conceptions of [order](/wiki/Political_order "Political order"), [power](/wiki/Power_(social_and_political) "Power (social and political)"), [liberty](/wiki/Liberty "Liberty"), [rights](/wiki/Rights "Rights"), [national development](/wiki/Economic_development "Economic development"), and [imperial autonomy](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"). Confederation's legacy remains debated, celebrated as the moment Canada's people assumed control of [its own development](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") and started on the course to [sovereignty](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982"), questioned for the limited place it left [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada"), and continually reinterpreted as constitutional debates over such things as the [nature of Canadian federalism](/wiki/Canadian_federalism "Canadian federalism") or [the character of the founding compact](/wiki/Constitutional_debate_in_Canada "Constitutional debate in Canada") reshape the understanding of its impact and significance.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

### Confederation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Confederation")]

Canada is a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) rather than a confederate association of sovereign states, which is what *[confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation")* means in contemporary political theory. The country, though, is often considered to be among the world's more [decentralized](/wiki/Decentralized "Decentralized") federations.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Use of the term *confederation* arose in the Province of Canada to refer to proposals beginning in the 1850s to federate all of the British North American colonies, as opposed to only Canada West (now Ontario) and Canada East (now Quebec). To contemporaries of Confederation, the *con-* prefix indicated a strengthening of the centrist principle compared to the American federation.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196237–38,_footnote_6-6)

In this Canadian context, *confederation* describes the political process that united the colonies in 1867, events related to that process, and the subsequent incorporation of other colonies and territories.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The word is now often used to describe Canada in an abstract way, such as in "the Fathers of Confederation"; provinces that became part of Canada after 1867 are also said to have [joined, or entered into, Confederation](#Joining_Confederation) (but not *the* Confederation).[[8]](#cite_note-8) The term is also used to divide [Canadian history](/wiki/Canadian_history "Canadian history") into pre-Confederation and post-Confederation periods.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Fathers of Confederation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Fathers of Confederation")]

Main article: [Fathers of Confederation](/wiki/Fathers_of_Confederation "Fathers of Confederation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/The_Founders_of_Confederation_of_the_Dominion_of_Canada_%28HS85-10-32966%29.jpg/250px-The_Founders_of_Confederation_of_the_Dominion_of_Canada_%28HS85-10-32966%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Founders_of_Confederation_of_the_Dominion_of_Canada_(HS85-10-32966).jpg)

The Fathers of Confederation

The original Fathers of Confederation are those delegates who attended any of the conferences held at Charlottetown and Quebec in 1864 or in London, United Kingdom, in 1866, leading to Confederation.[[10]](#cite_note-10) There were 36 original Fathers of Confederation; [Hewitt Bernard](/wiki/Hewitt_Bernard "Hewitt Bernard"), who was the recording secretary at the [Charlottetown Conference](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference"), is considered by some to be among them.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The individuals who brought the other provinces into Confederation after 1867 are also referred to as *Fathers of Confederation*. In this way, [Amor De Cosmos](/wiki/Amor_De_Cosmos "Amor De Cosmos"), who was instrumental both in bringing democracy to British Columbia and in bringing the province into Confederation, is considered to be a Father of Confederation.[[12]](#cite_note-12) As well, [Joey Smallwood](/wiki/Joey_Smallwood "Joey Smallwood") referred to himself as "the Last Father of Confederation" because he helped lead [Newfoundland](/wiki/Dominion_of_Newfoundland "Dominion of Newfoundland") into the union in 1949.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Constitutional history of Canada](/wiki/Constitutional_history_of_Canada "Constitutional history of Canada")

### Colonial organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Colonial organization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/68/William_Alexander%2C_1st_Earl_of_Stirling_-_Project_Gutenberg_etext_20110.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Alexander,_1st_Earl_of_Stirling_-_Project_Gutenberg_etext_20110.jpg)

Sir [William Alexander](/wiki/William_Alexander,_1st_Earl_of_Stirling "William Alexander, 1st Earl of Stirling")

All the [former colonies and territories](/wiki/Former_colonies_and_territories_in_Canada "Former colonies and territories in Canada") that became involved in the Canadian Confederation on July 1, 1867, were initially part of [New France](/wiki/New_France "New France"), and were once ruled by [France](/wiki/Early_modern_France "Early modern France").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDorinKaltembackRahal200714–17-14) [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") was granted in 1621 to [Sir William Alexander](/wiki/William_Alexander,_1st_Earl_of_Stirling "William Alexander, 1st Earl of Stirling") under [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter") by [James I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDorinKaltembackRahal200714–17-14) This claim overlapped the French claims to [Acadia](/wiki/Acadia "Acadia"), and although the [Scottish colony](/wiki/Scottish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Scottish colonization of the Americas") of Nova Scotia was short-lived, for political reasons, the conflicting imperial interests of France and the 18th century [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") led to a long and bitter struggle for control. The British acquired present-day mainland Nova Scotia by the [Treaty of Utrecht](/wiki/Treaty_of_Utrecht "Treaty of Utrecht") of 1713 and the [Acadian population was expelled](/wiki/Expulsion_of_the_Acadians "Expulsion of the Acadians") by the British in 1755. They renamed Acadia "Nova Scotia", which included present-day [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDorinKaltembackRahal200714–17-14) The rest of New France was acquired by the British as the result of its defeat of New France in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), which ended with the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1763) "Treaty of Paris (1763)") in 1763. From 1763 to 1791, most of New France became the [Province of Quebec](/wiki/Province_of_Quebec_(1763%E2%80%931791) "Province of Quebec (1763–1791)").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDorinKaltembackRahal200714–17-14) However, in 1769 the present-day [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"), which had been part of Acadia, was renamed "St John's Island" and organized as a separate colony.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESemple1996460-15) It was renamed "Prince Edward Island" in 1798 in honour of [Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESemple1996460-15)

The first [English attempt at settlement](/wiki/English_overseas_possessions "English overseas possessions") on that part of the continent that would become modern Canada had been in [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_Colony "Newfoundland Colony") which would not join Confederation until 1949.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes2006212-16) The [Society of Merchant Venturers](/wiki/Society_of_Merchant_Venturers "Society of Merchant Venturers") of [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") began to settle [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") at [Cuper's Cove](/wiki/Cuper%27s_Cove "Cuper's Cove") as far back as 1610, and Newfoundland had also been the subject of [a French colonial enterprise](/wiki/Plaisance,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Plaisance, Newfoundland and Labrador").[[17]](#cite_note-Clarke2010re-17)

During and after the [U.S. War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), an estimated 50,000 [United Empire Loyalists](/wiki/United_Empire_Loyalist "United Empire Loyalist") fled to [British North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDorinKaltembackRahal200714–17-14) The British created the separate province of New Brunswick in 1784 for Loyalists who settled in the western part of Nova Scotia.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes2006127-18) Nova Scotia (including New Brunswick) received slightly more than half of this influx, and many Loyalists settled in the province of Quebec, which later by the [Constitutional Act 1791](/wiki/Constitutional_Act_1791 "Constitutional Act 1791") was separated into a predominantly English [Upper Canada](/wiki/Upper_Canada "Upper Canada") and a predominantly French [Lower Canada](/wiki/Lower_Canada "Lower Canada").[[19]](#cite_note-FrancisFrancis2009a-19) The [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") and [Treaty of 1818](/wiki/Treaty_of_1818 "Treaty of 1818") established the border between British North America and the United States at the [49th parallel](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north") from the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") to the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") in Western Canada.[[20]](#cite_note-Kemp2010-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Canada_provinces_1867-1870.png/250px-Canada_provinces_1867-1870.png)](/wiki/File:Canada_provinces_1867-1870.png)

Canadian territory at Confederation

Following the [Rebellions of 1837](/wiki/Rebellions_of_1837 "Rebellions of 1837"), [Lord Durham](/wiki/John_Lambton,_1st_Earl_of_Durham "John Lambton, 1st Earl of Durham") in his [Durham Report](/wiki/Durham_Report "Durham Report"), recommended Upper and Lower Canada be joined as the [Province of Canada](/wiki/Province_of_Canada "Province of Canada") and the new province should have a [responsible government](/wiki/Responsible_government "Responsible government").[[21]](#cite_note-MatthewsGentilcore1987-21) As a result of Durham's report, the British Parliament passed the [Act of Union 1840](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1840 "Act of Union 1840"), and the Province of Canada was formed in 1841.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1999552-22) The new province had two parts: [Canada West](/wiki/Canada_West "Canada West") (the former Upper Canada, today's Ontario) and [Canada East](/wiki/Canada_East "Canada East") (the former Lower Canada, today's Quebec).[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagocsi1999552-22) Governor General [Lord Elgin](/wiki/James_Bruce,_8th_Earl_of_Elgin "James Bruce, 8th Earl of Elgin") granted ministerial responsibility in 1848, first to Nova Scotia and then to the Province of Canada. Later, the British parliament extended responsible government to Prince Edward Island (1851), New Brunswick (1854), and Newfoundland (1855).[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECareless1963205-23)

The area constituting modern-day [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") is the remnants of the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company")'s [Columbia District](/wiki/Columbia_District "Columbia District") and [New Caledonia District](/wiki/New_Caledonia_District "New Caledonia District") following the [Oregon Treaty](/wiki/Oregon_Treaty "Oregon Treaty"). Before joining Canada in 1871, British Columbia consisted of the separate [Colony of British Columbia](/wiki/Colony_of_British_Columbia_(1858%E2%80%931866) "Colony of British Columbia (1858–1866)") (formed in 1858, in an area where the Crown had granted a monopoly to the Hudson's Bay Company), and the [Colony of Vancouver Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Vancouver_Island "Colony of Vancouver Island") (formed in 1849) constituting a separate [crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") until it was united with the colony of British Columbia in 1866.[[24]](#cite_note-Whitaker1874-24)

The remainder of modern-day Canada was made up of [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land") and the [North-Western Territory](/wiki/North-Western_Territory "North-Western Territory") (both of which were controlled by the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") and sold to Canada in 1870) and the [Arctic Islands](/wiki/British_Arctic_Territories "British Arctic Territories"), which were under direct British control and became a part of Canada in 1880.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmmerson201073-25) Plus, Newfoundland and Labrador joined Canada in 1949.

### Early attempts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early attempts")]

The idea of joining the various colonies in North America was being floated as early as 1814. That year, [Chief Justice of Lower Canada](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_Quebec#Chief_Justice_of_Lower_Canada_(1791-1841) "Chief Justice of Quebec") [Jonathan Sewell](/wiki/Jonathan_Sewell "Jonathan Sewell") sent a copy of his report, *A Plan for the federal Union of British Provinces in North America*, to [Prince Edward](/wiki/Prince_Edward,_Duke_of_Kent_and_Strathearn "Prince Edward, Duke of Kent and Strathearn") (both a son of King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") and the father of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria")), whom Sewell had befriended when they both resided in [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). Edward replied, "nothing can be better arranged than the whole thing is, or more perfectly", going on to suggest a unified Canada consisting of two provinces—one formed from Upper and Lower Canada and the other from the Maritime colonies—each with a lieutenant governor and executive council, one located in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and the other in either [Annapolis Royal](/wiki/Annapolis_Royal "Annapolis Royal") or [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor,_Nova_Scotia "Windsor, Nova Scotia").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Edward said he would pass the report on to [the Earl Bathurst](/wiki/Henry_Bathurst,_3rd_Earl_Bathurst "Henry Bathurst, 3rd Earl Bathurst"), the then-[Secretary of State for War and the Colonies](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_War_and_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for War and the Colonies"); the Prince's comments and critiques were later cited by both the Earl of Durham and participants of the [Charlottetown](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference") and [Quebec Conferences](/wiki/Quebec_Conference,_1864 "Quebec Conference, 1864").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier.jpg/250px-George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier.jpg)](/wiki/File:George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier.jpg)

[George-Étienne Cartier](/wiki/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier "George-Étienne Cartier")

Lord Durham presented his idea of unification in 1839 *[Report on the Affairs of British North America](/wiki/Report_on_the_Affairs_of_British_North_America "Report on the Affairs of British North America")*,[[27]](#cite_note-KaufmanMacpherson2005tg-27) which resulted in the Act of Union 1840. Beginning in 1857, [Joseph-Charles Taché](/wiki/Joseph-Charles_Tach%C3%A9 "Joseph-Charles Taché") proposed a federation in a series of 33 articles published in the *Courrier du Canada*.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196240-28) Two years later, [Alexander Tilloch Galt](/wiki/Alexander_Tilloch_Galt "Alexander Tilloch Galt"), [George-Étienne Cartier](/wiki/George-%C3%89tienne_Cartier "George-Étienne Cartier"), and [John Ross](/wiki/John_Ross_(Canadian_politician) "John Ross (Canadian politician)") travelled to the United Kingdom to present the British Parliament with a project for confederation of the British colonies. The proposal was received by the London authorities with polite indifference.

The [royal tour of British North America](/wiki/Royal_tours_of_Canada#1860_royal_tour "Royal tours of Canada") undertaken by Queen Victoria's son, Prince Albert Edward (later King [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII")) in 1860, however, helped lead to the unification of the colonies by confirming a common bond between their inhabitants;[[29]](#cite_note-29) indeed, the monarchy played a "pivotal legal and symbolic role [...] in cementing the new Canadian union".[[30]](#cite_note-30) Further, by 1864, it was clear that continued governance of the Province of Canada under the terms of the 1840 Act of Union had become impracticable. Therefore, a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition "Grand coalition") of parties, the [Great Coalition](/wiki/Great_Coalition "Great Coalition"), formed in order to reform the political system.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196244-31) Queen Victoria remarked on "the impossibility of our being able to hold Canada; but, we must struggle for it; and by far the best solution would be to let it go as an independent kingdom under an English prince."[[32]](#cite_note-TCE-32)

### Influences leading to Confederation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Influences leading to Confederation")]

Several factors influenced Confederation, caused both by internal sources and pressures from external sources.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Internally, there was political deadlock resulting from the contemporary governmental structure in the Province of Canada and distrust between English Protestants and French Catholics.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Further, demographic pressure from an expanding population and economic nationalism wanting economic development butted against a lack of an inter-colonial railroad, which hampered trade, military movement, and transportation in general.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Externally, the [Canadian–American Reciprocity Treaty](/wiki/Canadian%E2%80%93American_Reciprocity_Treaty "Canadian–American Reciprocity Treaty") (a free trade policy, starting in 1854, whereby products were allowed into the United States without taxes or tariffs, which was then considered to be beneficial for Canada) was cancelled by the United States in 1865, partly as revenge against Britain for unofficial support of the south in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). Additionally, the U.S. doctrine of "[manifest destiny](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny")" raised fears of another American invasion (Canadians had fended off American incursions during the Revolutionary War, War of 1812, [Fenian raids](/wiki/Fenian_raids "Fenian raids"), and [St. Albans Raid](/wiki/St._Albans_Raid "St. Albans Raid")[[39]](#cite_note-39)), only further inflamed by the [Alaska Purchase](/wiki/Alaska_Purchase "Alaska Purchase") of March 30, 1867, which had been supported in the U.S. Senate (by [Charles Sumner](/wiki/Charles_Sumner "Charles Sumner") among others) precisely in terms of taking the remainder of North America from the British. The American Civil War had also horrified Canadians and turned many from the thought of republicanism.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In Britain, political pressure came from financiers who had lost money by investing in the failed [Grand Trunk Railway](/wiki/Grand_Trunk_Railway "Grand Trunk Railway") and the [little Englander](/wiki/Little_Englander "Little Englander") philosophy fed a desire to withdraw troops from Britain's colonies.

### Ideological origins and philosophical dimensions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ideological origins and philosophical dimensions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Map_of_the_Eastern_British_Provinces_in_North_America_at_the_time_of_Confederation_1867.jpg/250px-Map_of_the_Eastern_British_Provinces_in_North_America_at_the_time_of_Confederation_1867.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Eastern_British_Provinces_in_North_America_at_the_time_of_Confederation_1867.jpg)

Map of the Eastern British Provinces in North America at the time of Canadian Confederation, 1867

There is extensive scholarly debate on the role of political ideas in Canadian Confederation. Traditionally, historians regarded Canadian Confederation an exercise in political pragmatism that was essentially non-ideological. In the 1960s, historian [Peter Waite](/wiki/Peter_Busby_Waite "Peter Busby Waite") derided the references to political philosophers in the legislative debates on Confederation as "hot air". In Waite's view, Confederation was driven by pragmatic brokerage politics and competing interest groups.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

In 1987, political scientist Peter J. Smith challenged the view Canadian Confederation was non-ideological. Smith argued Confederation was motivated by new political ideologies as much as the American and French Revolutions and Canadian Confederation was driven by a [Court Party](/wiki/Court_Party_(Canada) "Court Party (Canada)") ideology. Smith traces the origins of this ideology to eighteenth and nineteenth-century Britain, where political life was polarized between defenders of [classical republican](/wiki/Classical_republicanism "Classical republicanism") values of the [Country Party](/wiki/Country_Party_(Britain) "Country Party (Britain)") and proponents of a new pro-capitalist ideology of the Court Party, which believed in centralizing political power. In British North America in the late 1860s, the Court Party tradition was represented by the supporters of Confederation, whereas the anti-capitalist and agrarian Country Party tradition was embodied by the Anti-Confederates.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In a 2000 journal article, historian [Ian McKay](/wiki/Ian_McKay_(historian) "Ian McKay (historian)") argued Canadian Confederation was motivated by the ideology of liberalism and the belief in the supremacy of individual rights. McKay described Confederation as part of the classical liberal project of creating a "liberal order" in northern North America.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Many Canadian historians have adopted McKay's liberal order framework as a paradigm for understanding Canadian history.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

In 2008, historian Andrew Smith advanced a very different view of Confederation's ideological origins. He argues that in the four original Canadian provinces, the politics of taxation were a central issue in the debate about Confederation. Taxation was also central to the debate in Newfoundland, the tax-averse colony that rejected it. Smith argued Confederation was supported by many colonists who were sympathetic to a relatively interventionist, or statist, approach to capitalist development. Most classical liberals, who believed in free trade and low taxes, opposed Confederation because they feared it would result in Big Government. The struggle over Confederation involved a battle between a staunch individualist economic philosophy and a comparatively collectivist view of the state's proper role in the economy. According to Smith, the victory of the [statist](/wiki/Statism "Statism") supporters of Confederation over their [anti-statist](/wiki/Anti-statist "Anti-statist") opponents prepared the way for [John A. Macdonald](/wiki/John_A._Macdonald "John A. Macdonald")'s government to enact the protectionist National Policy and to subsidize major infrastructure projects such as the [Intercolonial](/wiki/Intercolonial_Railway "Intercolonial Railway") and [Pacific](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") Railways.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In 2007, political scientist [Janet Ajzenstat](/wiki/Janet_Ajzenstat "Janet Ajzenstat") connected Canadian Confederation to the individualist ideology of [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"). She argued that the union of the British North American colonies was motivated by a desire to protect individual rights, especially the rights to life, liberty, and property. She contends the Fathers of Confederation were motivated by the values of the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. She argues their intellectual debts to Locke are most evident when one looks at the 1865 debates in the Province of Canada's legislature on whether or not union with the other British North American colonies would be desirable.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Charlottetown Conference

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Charlottetown Conference")]

Main article: [Charlottetown Conference](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference")

In the spring of 1864, [New Brunswick premier](/wiki/New_Brunswick_premier "New Brunswick premier") [Samuel Leonard Tilley](/wiki/Samuel_Leonard_Tilley "Samuel Leonard Tilley"), [Nova Scotia premier](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_premier "Nova Scotia premier") [Charles Tupper](/wiki/Charles_Tupper "Charles Tupper"), and [Prince Edward Island premier](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island_premier "Prince Edward Island premier") [John Hamilton Gray](/wiki/John_Hamilton_Gray_(Prince_Edward_Island_politician) "John Hamilton Gray (Prince Edward Island politician)") were contemplating the idea of a [Maritime Union](/wiki/Maritime_Union "Maritime Union") which would join their three colonies together.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196256-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Charlottetown_Conference_Delegates%2C_September_1864.JPG/250px-Charlottetown_Conference_Delegates%2C_September_1864.JPG)](/wiki/File:Charlottetown_Conference_Delegates,_September_1864.JPG)

Delegates of the [Charlottetown Conference](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference") on the steps of [Government House](/wiki/Government_House_(Prince_Edward_Island) "Government House (Prince Edward Island)"), September 1864

The government of the Province of Canada surprised the Maritime governments by asking if the Province of Canada could be included in the negotiations. The request was channelled through the governor general, Monck, to London and accepted by the Colonial Office.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008-48) After several years of legislative paralysis in the Province of Canada caused by the need to maintain a double legislative majority (a majority of both the Canada East and Canada West delegates in the Province of Canada's legislature), Macdonald had led his [Liberal-Conservative Party](/wiki/Liberal-Conservative_Party "Liberal-Conservative Party") into the Great Coalition with Cartier's *[Parti bleu](/wiki/Parti_bleu "Parti bleu")* and [George Brown](/wiki/George_Brown_(Canadian_politician) "George Brown (Canadian politician)")'s [Clear Grits](/wiki/Clear_Grits "Clear Grits").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECareless1963233-49) Macdonald, Cartier, and Brown felt union with the other British colonies might be a way to solve the political problems of the Province of Canada.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECareless1963233-49)

The [Charlottetown Conference](/wiki/Charlottetown_Conference "Charlottetown Conference") began on September 1, 1864. Since the agenda for the meeting had already been set, the delegation from the Province of Canada was initially not an official part of the Conference. The issue of Maritime Union was deferred and the Canadians were formally allowed to join and address the Conference.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196283-50)

No minutes from the Charlottetown Conference survive, but it is known Cartier and Macdonald presented arguments in favour of a union of the three colonies,[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008304-51) Alexander Tilloch Galt presented the Province of Canada's proposals on the financial arrangements of such a union,[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008304-51) and George Brown presented a proposal for what form a united government might take.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196287-52) The Canadian delegation's proposal for the governmental system involved:

1. preservation of ties with Great Britain
2. residual jurisdiction left to a central authority
3. a bicameral system including a Lower House with representation by population (rep by pop) and an Upper House with representation based on regional, rather than provincial, equality
4. responsible government at the federal and provincial levels
5. the appointment of a [Canadian governor general](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") by the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown")

Other proposals attractive to the politicians from the Maritime colonies were:

1. assumption of provincial debt by the central government[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196285-53)
2. revenues from the central government apportioned to the provinces on the basis of population[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196285-53)
3. the building of an intercolonial railway to link Montreal and Halifax, giving Canada access to an ice-free winter port and the Maritimes easy access to Canada and [Rupert's Land](/wiki/Rupert%27s_Land "Rupert's Land")[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008307-54)

By September 7, 1864, the delegates from Nova Scotia, New Brunswick and Prince Edward Island gave a positive answer to the Canadian delegation, expressing the view the federation of all of the provinces was considered desirable if the terms of union could be made satisfactory[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008305-55) and the question of Maritime Union was waived.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196287-52)

After the Conference adjourned on September 9, there were further meetings between delegates held at [Halifax](/wiki/Halifax,_Nova_Scotia "Halifax, Nova Scotia"), [Saint John](/wiki/Saint_John,_New_Brunswick "Saint John, New Brunswick"), and [Fredericton](/wiki/Fredericton "Fredericton").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196288-56)[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008306-57) These meetings evinced enough interest that the delegates decided to hold a second Conference.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/ThomasDArcyMcGee.jpg/250px-ThomasDArcyMcGee.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThomasDArcyMcGee.jpg)

Thomas D'Arcy McGee in 1868

#### Delegates' reactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Delegates' reactions")]

One of the most important purposes of the Charlottetown Conference was the introduction of Canadians to the leaders from the Maritime Provinces and vice versa. At this point, there was no railway link from Quebec City to Halifax, and the people of each region had little to do with one another. [Thomas D'Arcy McGee](/wiki/Thomas_D%27Arcy_McGee "Thomas D'Arcy McGee") was one of the few Canadian delegates who had been to the Maritimes, when he had gone down earlier that summer with a trade mission of Canadian businessmen, journalists and politicians.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008306-57)

George Brown remarked in a letter to his wife Anne that at a party given by the premier of PEI, Colonel John Hamilton Gray, he met a woman who had never been off the island in her entire life. Nevertheless, he found Prince Edward Islanders to be "amazingly civilized".[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008305-55)

#### Press and popular reaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Press and popular reaction")]

Reaction to the Charlottetown Conference varied among the different newspapers. In the Maritimes, there was concern that the smooth Canadians with their sparkling champagne and charming speeches were outsmarting the delegates of the smaller provinces. "From all accounts it looks as if these [Canadian] gentlemen had it all their own way; ... and that, what with their arguments and what with their blandishments, (they gave a champagne lunch on board the *Victoria* where Mr. McGee's wit sparkled brightly as the wine), they carried the Lower Province delegates a little off their feet."[[58]](#cite_note-58)

The delegates from the Quebec conference considered if the resolutions would be better suited for acceptance if a popular vote were held on them. However, due to the divide amongst religious groups and general mistrust between areas in Canada, they believed that such a vote would be defeated. Thus, they went ahead with the resolutions on their own volition.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Quebec Conference

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Quebec Conference")]

Main articles: [Quebec Conference, 1864](/wiki/Quebec_Conference,_1864 "Quebec Conference, 1864") and [Quebec Resolutions](/wiki/Quebec_Resolutions "Quebec Resolutions")

After returning home from the Charlottetown Conference, Macdonald asked [Viscount Monck](/wiki/Charles_Monck,_4th_Viscount_Monck "Charles Monck, 4th Viscount Monck"), the [governor general of the Province of Canada](/wiki/Governor-General_of_the_Province_of_Canada "Governor-General of the Province of Canada") to invite delegates from the three Maritime provinces and Newfoundland to a conference with United Canada delegates. At the opening of the conference, a total of 33 delegates were included from the British North American Colonies, including Newfoundland, which had not participated in prior meetings.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Monck obliged and the Conference went ahead at [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") in October 1864.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/QuebecConvention1864.jpg/250px-QuebecConvention1864.jpg)](/wiki/File:QuebecConvention1864.jpg)

Delegates at the [Quebec Conference](/wiki/Quebec_Conference,_1864 "Quebec Conference, 1864"), October 1864

The Conference began on October 10, 1864, on the site of present-day Montmorency Park.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The Conference elected [Étienne-Paschal Taché](/wiki/%C3%89tienne-Paschal_Tach%C3%A9 "Étienne-Paschal Taché") as its chairman, but it was dominated by Macdonald. Despite differences in the positions of some of the delegates on some issues, the Quebec Conference, following so swiftly on the success of the Charlottetown Conference, was infused with a determinative sense of purpose and [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196298-62) For the Reformers of Canada West, led by George Brown, the end of what they perceived as French-Canadian interference in local affairs was in sight.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196299-63) For Maritimers such as Tupper of Nova Scotia or Tilley of New Brunswick, horizons were suddenly broadened to take in much larger possibilities for trade and growth.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite196299-63)

On the issue of the Senate, the Maritime Provinces pressed for as much equality as possible. With the addition of Newfoundland to the Conference, the three Maritime colonies did not wish to see the strength of their provinces in the upper chamber diluted by simply adding Newfoundland to the Atlantic category.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite1962100-64) It was the matter of the Senate that threatened to derail the entire proceedings.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008317-65) It was Macdonald who came up with the acceptable compromise of giving Newfoundland four senators of its own when it joined.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008317-65)

The delegates from the Maritimes also raised an issue with respect to the level of government—federal or provincial—that would be given the powers not otherwise specifically defined. Macdonald, who was aiming for the strongest central government possible, insisted this was to be the central government, and in this, he was supported by, among others, Tupper.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite1962105-66)

At the end of the Conference, it adopted the "[seventy-two resolutions](/wiki/Seventy-two_resolutions "Seventy-two resolutions")" which would form the basis of a scheduled future conference. The Conference adjourned on October 27.

Prince Edward Island emerged disappointed from the Quebec Conference. It did not receive support for a guarantee of six members in the proposed House of Commons, and was denied an appropriation of $200,000 it felt had been offered at Charlottetown to assist in [buying out the holdings of absentee landlords](/wiki/Land_Purchase_Act_(1875) "Land Purchase Act (1875)").[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaite1962107-67)

#### Press and popular reaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Press and popular reaction")]

"Never was there such an opportunity as now for the birth of a nation" proclaimed a pamphlet written by S. E. Dawson and reprinted in a Quebec City newspaper during the Conference.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGwyn2008317-65)

Again, reaction to the Quebec Conference varied depending on the political views of the critic.

#### Constitutional scheme discussed in London

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Constitutional scheme discussed in London")]

George Brown was the first, in December 1864, to carry the constitutional proposals to the British government in London, where Brown received "a most gracious answer to our constitutional scheme".[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackenzie1892230-68) He also met with William Gladstone—who was then Chancellor of the Exchequer and, later, Prime Minister—"who agreed in almost everything".[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackenzie1892230-68) In April 1865, Brown, Macdonald, Cartier and Galt met with the government and found "the project of a federal union of the colonies was highly approved of by the imperial authorities".[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackenzie189296–97-69)

On the form of the proposed system of governance for Canada, the Fathers of Confederation were influenced by the American republic. Macdonald said in 1865:

> By adhering to the monarchical principle, we avoid one defect inherent in the [constitution of the United States](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"). By the election of the president by a majority and for a short period, he never is the sovereign and chief of the nation. He is never looked up to by the whole people as the head and front of the nation. He is, at best, but the successful leader of a party. This defect is all the greater on account of the practice of reelection. During his first term of office, he is employed in taking steps to secure his own reelection and, for his party, a continuance of power. We avoid this by adhering to the monarchical principle—the sovereign, whom you respect and love. I believe that it is of the utmost importance to have that principle recognized so that we shall have a sovereign who is placed above the region of party—to whom all parties look up; who is not elevated by the action of one party nor depressed by the action of another; who is the common head and sovereign of all.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### London Conference

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: London Conference")]

Main article: [London Conference of 1866](/wiki/London_Conference_of_1866 "London Conference of 1866")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Queen_Victoria_1887.jpg/250px-Queen_Victoria_1887.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen_Victoria_1887.jpg)

[Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") granted [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") to the *[British North America Act](/wiki/British_North_America_Act_(1867) "British North America Act (1867)")* on March 29, 1867

Following the Quebec Conference, the Province of Canada's legislature passed a bill approving the union. The union proved more controversial in the Maritime provinces, however, and it was not until 1866 that New Brunswick and Nova Scotia passed union resolutions, while Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland continued to opt against joining.

In December 1866, sixteen delegates from the Province of Canada, New Brunswick, and Nova Scotia travelled to London, where [the Earl of Carnarvon](/wiki/Henry_Herbert,_4th_Earl_of_Carnarvon "Henry Herbert, 4th Earl of Carnarvon") presented each to [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") in private [audience](/wiki/Audience_(meeting) "Audience (meeting)"),[[71]](#cite_note-Lady_Carnarvon-71) as well as holding court for their wives and daughters.[[72]](#cite_note-72) To the Nova Scotian delegates, the Queen said, "I take the deepest interest in [Confederation], for I believe it will make [the provinces] great and prosperous."[[73]](#cite_note-73)

At meetings held at the Westminster Palace Hotel, the delegates reviewed and approved the 72 resolutions; although Charles Tupper had promised to anti-union forces in Nova Scotia that he would push for amendments, he was unsuccessful in getting any passed. Now known as the *London Resolutions*, the conference's decisions were forwarded to the [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office").

After breaking for Christmas, the delegates reconvened in January 1867 and began drafting the *[British North America Act](/wiki/British_North_America_Act_(1867) "British North America Act (1867)")*. The 4th Earl of Carnarvon continued to have a central role in drafting the act at [Highclere Castle](/wiki/Highclere_Castle "Highclere Castle") alongside the first [prime minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") Macdonald, Cartier and Galt, who signed the visitor book in 1866.[[74]](#cite_note-74) After suggestions of 'Franklin' and 'Guelfenland',[[71]](#cite_note-Lady_Carnarvon-71) they agreed the new country should be called *Canada*, Canada East should be renamed *Quebec* and Canada West should be renamed *Ontario*.[[75]](#cite_note-OlsonShadle1996oi-75) There was, however, heated debate about how the new country should be designated. Ultimately, the delegates elected to call the new country the Dominion of Canada, after "kingdom" and "confederation", among other options, were rejected. The term *dominion* was allegedly suggested by Sir Samuel Leonard Tilley.[[76]](#cite_note-Rayburn2001qwe-76)

The delegates had completed their draft of the *British North America Act* by February 1867. The act was presented to Queen Victoria on February 11, 1867. The bill was introduced in the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") the next day. The bill was quickly approved by the House of Lords, and then also quickly approved by the [British House of Commons](/wiki/British_House_of_Commons "British House of Commons"). (The [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") [Lord Derby](/wiki/Edward_Smith-Stanley,_14th_Earl_of_Derby "Edward Smith-Stanley, 14th Earl of Derby") was [prime minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") at the time.) The act received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") on March 29, 1867, and set July 1, 1867, as the date for union.[[77]](#cite_note-Moore2011-77)

### British North America Acts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: British North America Acts")]

Main article: [British North America Acts](/wiki/British_North_America_Acts "British North America Acts")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Proclamation_Canadian_Confederation.jpg/250px-Proclamation_Canadian_Confederation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Proclamation_Canadian_Confederation.jpg)

The proclamation of Canadian Confederation

Confederation was accomplished when the Queen gave royal assent to the *British North America Act* (*BNA Act*) on March 29, 1867, followed by a royal proclamation stating, "we do ordain, declare, and command that, on and after the first day of July, one thousand eight hundred and sixty-seven, the provinces of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick shall form and be One Dominion, under the name of Canada."[[78]](#cite_note-78) The act replaced the Act of Union 1840, which had unified Upper Canada and Lower Canada into the united Province of Canada; separate provinces were established under their current names of Ontario and Quebec, respectively. July 1 is now celebrated as [Canada Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day"), the country's official [national day](/wiki/National_day "National day").

Confederation is regarded as the creation of a kingdom in its own right[[82]](#cite_note-82) and to have "successfully reconciled the physical absence of a geographically distant monarch with a continuing and pervasive presence through the medium of formal representatives and the manner and forms of legal and conventional rules and behaviour associated with British parliamentary and monarchical governance".[[83]](#cite_note-83) Macdonald had spoken of "founding a great British monarchy" and wanted the newly created country to be called the *Kingdom of Canada*.[[84]](#cite_note-84) The [Colonial Office](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_the_Colonies "Secretary of State for the Colonies") opposed the term *kingdom* as "premature" and "pretentious" and felt it might antagonize the United States. The term *[dominion](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion")* was chosen, instead, to indicate Canada's status as a self-governing polity of the British Empire, the first time it was used in reference to a country.[[85]](#cite_note-85) When the *British North America Act, 1867*, was passed in the Parliament in [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster "Westminster"), the Queen said to Macdonald, "I am very glad to see you on this mission [...] It is a very important measure and you have all exhibited so much loyalty."[[32]](#cite_note-TCE-32)

While the *BNA Act* eventually resulted in Canada having more autonomy than it had before, the country was still far from fully independent of the United Kingdom. Foreign policy remained in British hands, the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") remained Canada's highest court of appeal, and the constitution could be amended only in Britain. Gradually, Canada gained more autonomy; defence of British North America became a Canadian responsibility.[[86]](#cite_note-Dyck2011b-86) According to the Supreme Court of Canada, Canadian "sovereignty was acquired in the period between its separate signature of the Treaty of Versailles in 1919 and the Statute of Westminster, 1931",[[87]](#cite_note-87) which gave the country nearly full independence. It was only because the federal and provincial governments were unable to agree on a formula for amending the constitution that the power to do so remained with the British Parliament. Once that issue was resolved, the constitution was [patriated](/wiki/Patriation "Patriation") when [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") gave royal assent to the [Canada Act, 1982](/wiki/Canada_Act,_1982 "Canada Act, 1982").

The [constitution of Canada](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada") is made up of a number of codified acts and uncodified conventions; one of the principal documents is the *[Constitution Act, 1982](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1982 "Constitution Act, 1982")*, which renamed the *British North America Act, 1867*, to the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*.[[88]](#cite_note-Okafọ2009-88)[[89]](#cite_note-CA1982-89) The act also details how power is distributed in both the provincial and federal jurisdictions. Two of the most important sections are 91 and 92. Section 91 gives Parliament jurisdiction over banking, interest rates, criminal law, the postal system, and the armed forces. Section 92 gives the provinces jurisdiction over property, contracts and torts, local works, and general business. Still, federal and provincial law may occasionally interfere with each other, in which case federal law prevails.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Results")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/JaMAC.jpg/250px-JaMAC.jpg)](/wiki/File:JaMAC.jpg)

John A. Macdonald became the first [prime minister of Canada](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada").

Dominion elections were held in August and September, 1867, to elect the first [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Canada "Parliament of Canada"). The governments of the Province of Canada, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick nominated the seventy-two individuals who would be named to the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada"), who were listed in the [proclamation of Confederation](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Constitution_Act,_1867 "Proclamation of the Constitution Act, 1867") (twenty-four each for Quebec and Ontario, twelve each for New Brunswick and Nova Scotia). They were called to the Senate by the governor general for the opening of the first Parliament.[[91]](#cite_note-Chambers's_encyclopaedia-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The Anti-Confederation Party won 18 out of 19 federal Nova Scotia seats in September 1867, and in the Nova Scotia provincial election of 1868, 36 out of 38 seats in the legislature. For seven years, William Annand and Joseph Howe led the ultimately unsuccessful fight to convince British imperial authorities to release Nova Scotia from Confederation. The government was vocally against Confederation, contending it was no more than the annexation of the province to the pre-existing province of Canada.[[93]](#cite_note-FrancisFrancis2009d-93)

Prior to the coming into effect of the *Constitution Act, 1867*, there had been some concern regarding a potential "legislative vacuum" that would occur over the 15-month period between the prorogation of the Province of Canada's final Parliament in August 1866 and the opening of the now Dominion of Canada's first Parliament in November 1867.[[94]](#cite_note-94) To prevent this, the *Constitution Act, 1867*, provided for "continuance of existing laws" from the three colonies of Canada, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick until new laws could be established in the Dominion.[[95]](#cite_note-95) Thus, the "Dominion's financial systems, structures and actors were able to operate under the provisions of the old Province of Canada Acts" following confederation, and many institutions and organizations were continued and assumed "the same responsibilities for the new federal government that it had held as a provincial organization".[[96]](#cite_note-baker-96)

## Joining Confederation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Joining Confederation")]

After the initial *BNA Act* in 1867, [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba") was established by an act of the Canadian Parliament on July 15, 1870, originally as an area of land much smaller than the current province.[[97]](#cite_note-Sprague1988-97) British Columbia joined Canada July 20, 1871, by an Imperial order-in-council enacted under the authority of the *British North America Act*.[[98]](#cite_note-Murphy1993-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100) The order-in-council incorporated the Terms of Union negotiated by the governments of Canada and British Columbia, including a commitment by the federal government to build a railway connecting British Columbia to the railway system of Canada within 10 years of union.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Prince Edward Island (PEI) joined July 1, 1873, also by an Imperial order-in-council.[[102]](#cite_note-102) One reason for joining was financial: PEI's economy was performing poorly and union would bring monetary benefits that would assist the province in avoiding bankruptcy.[[103]](#cite_note-lop.parl.ca-103) One of the Prince Edward Island Terms of Union was a guarantee by the federal government to operate a [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") link, a term deleted upon completion of the [Confederation Bridge](/wiki/Confederation_Bridge "Confederation Bridge") in 1997.[[98]](#cite_note-Murphy1993-98) [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan") were established September 1, 1905, by acts of the Canadian Parliament. Newfoundland joined on March 31, 1949, by an act of the Imperial Parliament, also with a ferry link guaranteed.[[98]](#cite_note-Murphy1993-98)[[104]](#cite_note-104)

The Crown acquired Rupert's Land and the North-Western Territory from the Hudson's Bay Company in 1869 (though final payment to the Hudson's Bay Company did not occur until 1870), and then transferred jurisdiction to the Dominion on July 15, 1870, merging them and naming them [North-West Territories](/wiki/North-West_Territories "North-West Territories").[[105]](#cite_note-Dominion_Lands_Policy-105) In 1880, the British assigned all [North American Arctic islands](/wiki/North_American_Arctic_islands "North American Arctic islands") to Canada, right up to [Ellesmere Island](/wiki/Ellesmere_Island "Ellesmere Island").[[106]](#cite_note-Diubaldo1999-106) From this vast swath of territory were created three provinces (Manitoba, Saskatchewan, Alberta) and two territories ([Yukon Territory](/wiki/Yukon_Territory "Yukon Territory") and North-West Territories, now Yukon and Northwest Territories), and two extensions each to Quebec, Ontario, and Manitoba. Later, the third territory of [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") was carved from the Northwest Territories on April 1, 1999.[[107]](#cite_note-DahlHicks2000-107) The Yukon territory was formed during the Klondike gold rush. People from all around Canada and the United States flocked to the area due to rumours of an easy way to get rich. The Canadian government sought to regulate this migration and tax gold findings, whether American or Canadian.[[103]](#cite_note-lop.parl.ca-103)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Canadian_Confederation&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Legacy")]

The term *confederation* has entered into Canadian parlance both as a metaphor for the country and for the historical events that created it. It has therefore become one of the most common names for Canadian landmarks. Examples include [Mount Confederation](/wiki/Mount_Confederation "Mount Confederation"), [Confederation Square](/wiki/Confederation_Square "Confederation Square"), the [Confederation Building](/wiki/Confederation_Building_(disambiguation) "Confederation Building (disambiguation)"), [Confederation Park](/wiki/Confederation_Park_(disambiguation) "Confederation Park (disambiguation)"), [Confederation Station](/wiki/Confederation_Station_(OC_Transpo) "Confederation Station (OC Transpo)"), [Confederation Heights](/wiki/Confederation_Heights "Confederation Heights"), Confederation Bridge, and so on. This is similar to the American practices of naming things *union* and likewise the Australians with *federation*.[*[opinion](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view/FAQ#Assert_facts,_not_opinions "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view/FAQ")*]

Indigenous communities were ignored in the process of Canadian confederation.[[109]](#cite_note-113) As a result of Confederation, the Parliament and government of Canada assumed the responsibilities of their British counterparts in treaty dealings with the First Nations. The federal Parliament subsequently passed the *[Indian Act](/wiki/Indian_Act "Indian Act")* in 1876, which, in amended form, continues to govern Indigenous peoples. Confederation created conditions of colonialism, including resource grabbing, broken treaties, forced assimilation, patriarchy, and intergenerational trauma inflicted by the hegemony of the Canadian state on Indigenous nations that had been self-governing.[[114]](#cite_note-118)

As the 20th century progressed, attention to the conditions of Indigenous peoples in Canada increased, which included the granting of full voting rights in 1960. Treaty rights were enshrined in the Canadian constitution in 1982 and,[[115]](#cite_note-119)[[116]](#cite_note-120) in *[Sparrow v. The Queen](/wiki/R._v._Sparrow "R. v. Sparrow")*,[[117]](#cite_note-121) the Supreme Court determined there exists a [fiduciary](/wiki/Fiduciary "Fiduciary") [affiliation between the Canadian Crown and Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada_and_the_Indigenous_peoples_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada and the Indigenous peoples of Canada") in which the Crown is constitutionally charged with providing certain guarantees to the First Nations.[[121]](#cite_note-125) Recognizing the principle of [aboriginal title](/wiki/Aboriginal_title "Aboriginal title"), a process of land claims settlements is ongoing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Created to resolve the effects of the residential school system, a [Truth and Reconciliation Commission](/wiki/Truth_and_Reconciliation_Commission_of_Canada "Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada") was struck to identify further measures to improve conditions.[[122]](#cite_note-126)
